coredata.dc:title: Enhanced electrochemical performance of carbon quantum dots-polyaniline hybrid
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Zhichao
Xie, Yibing
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots-polyaniline (CQDs-PANI) hybrid was developed as supercapacitor electorde material by incorporating CQDs into PANI. PANI nanowires were grown vertically on carbon fiber substrate to form an interconnected network structure. Meanwhile, CQDs were uniformly distributed in the interior and on the surface of well-established net-like PANI nanowires. High specific capacitance of 738.3 F g−1 at 1.0 A g−1 was obtained for CQDs-PANI compared to that of 432.5 F g−1 for pure PANI. The capacitance retention after 1000 cycles of CQDs-PANI and PANI is 78.0% and 68.0% at 5.0 A g−1, respectively. The high capacitance and reasonable cycle stability were ascribed to the incorporation of CQDs into PANI, which improved the conductivity and alleviated the volume change of the CQDs-PANI electrode during the charge/discharge process. In addition, a flexible solid-state CQDs-PANI supercapacitor was constructed using carbon paper as current collector and polyvinyl alcohol gel electrolyte, exhibiting the stable capacitive performance at planar and bending state. The specific capacitance, energy and power density were determined to be 169.2 mF cm−2, 33.8 μWh cm−2 and 0.3 mW cm−2 at a potential window of 1.2 V and a current density of 1.0 mA cm−2. CQDs-PANI presented the promising application in flexible energy-related device.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Polyaniline
Conductivity
Supercapacitor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrations of electrochemical fabrication of (A) CQDs and (B) CQDs-PANI/CF(LI).

(A) Photographs of CQDs suspension (a) before filtration; (b) after filtration; (c) TEM image of CQDs; (B) Photographs of (a) PANI/CFs, (b) PANI/CFs(LI), (c) CQDs-PANI/CFs and (d) CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI); (C) Schematic illustrations and corresponding SEM images of the CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) prepared by CV electrodeposition at different cycles of (a) 2 cycles, (b) 5 cycles and (c) 10 cycles.
SEM images of (A, B) PANI/CFs; (C, D) PANI/CFs(LI); (E, F) CQDs-PANI/CFs and (G, H) CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) with different magnifications.

(A) XRD patterns of KH2PO4, CQDs-KH2PO4, PANI and CQDs-PANI; (B) Fluorescent spectra; (C) Raman spectra of (a) CQDs, (b) PANI, (c) PANI(LI), (d) CQDs-PANI and (e) CQDs-PANI(LI); (D) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms of (a) PANI, (b) CQDs-PANI and (c) CQDs-PANI (LI); (E) Pore size distribution curves of (a) PANI, (b) CQDs-PANI and (c) CQDs-PANI (LI).

(A–C) Electrochemical performance of PANI/CFs, PANI/CFs(LI), CQDs-PANI/CFs and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI): (A) CV curves at 10 mV s-1; (B) GCD curves at 1.0 A g-1; (C) Specific capacitances at different current densities; (D–F) Electrochemical performance of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI): (D) CV curves at different scan rates; (E) GCD curves at current densities of 1.0–10.0 A g-1 and (F) Ragone plots; (G) CV curves of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) electrode in the potential range of −0.6–0 V at different scan rates of 5–50 mV s−1 (The inset shows the GCD curves in the potential window of −0.6–0 V at different current densities of 1.0–3.0 A g-1); (H) Cycling performance of all samples at 5.0 A g−1; (I) Raman spectra of (a) fresh CQDs-PANI(LI) and (b) CQDs-PANI(LI)-cycled; (J) Nyquist plots of all samples, the inset shows the equivalent circuit.

(A) CV curves of the all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor based on the CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) in the potential range of −1.0–1.0 V at different scan rates; (B) CV curves of CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) and PANI/CP at 20 mV s−1, the inset shows the supercapacitor prototypes of CQDs-PANI/CP(LI); (C) GCD curves of CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) and PANI/CP at 1.0 A cm−2; (D) CV curves of CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) at planar state and bending state; insets show the all-solid-state supercapacitor prototypes of CQDs-PANI/CP (LI) at (a) planar state, (b) bending state; (E–G) Electrochemical performance of CQDs-PANI/CP(LI): (E) CV curves at different scan rates; (F) GCD curves at different current densities; (G) Ragone plots, inset shows an all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor prototype using two symmetrical CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) hybrid electrodes and a PVA gel electrolyte and its powering an LED with a rated forward voltage of 1.8 V; (H) Nyquist plots of the supercapacitor based on CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) (The insets show the corresponding equivalent circuit and the enlarged Nyquist plots in high frequency region.).
The specific capacitances of CFs, CQDs/CFs, PANI/CFs, PANI/CFs(LI), CQDs-PANI/CFs and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) at various current densities.
The fitting values of the equivalent circuit elements of PANI/CFs, PANI/CFs(LI), CQDs-PANI/CFs and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI).

head.ce:title: Enhanced electrochemical performance of carbon quantum dots-polyaniline hybrid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 211189, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots-polyaniline (CQDs-PANI) hybrid was developed as supercapacitor electorde material by incorporating CQDs into PANI. PANI nanowires were grown vertically on carbon fiber substrate to form an interconnected network structure. Meanwhile, CQDs were uniformly distributed in the interior and on the surface of well-established net-like PANI nanowires. High specific capacitance of 738.3 F g−1 at 1.0 A g−1 was obtained for CQDs-PANI compared to that of 432.5 F g−1 for pure PANI. The capacitance retention after 1000 cycles of CQDs-PANI and PANI is 78.0% and 68.0% at 5.0 A g−1, respectively. The high capacitance and reasonable cycle stability were ascribed to the incorporation of CQDs into PANI, which improved the conductivity and alleviated the volume change of the CQDs-PANI electrode during the charge/discharge process. In addition, a flexible solid-state CQDs-PANI supercapacitor was constructed using carbon paper as current collector and polyvinyl alcohol gel electrolyte, exhibiting the stable capacitive performance at planar and bending state. The specific capacitance, energy and power density were determined to be 169.2 mF cm−2, 33.8 μWh cm−2 and 0.3 mW cm−2 at a potential window of 1.2 V and a current density of 1.0 mA cm−2. CQDs-PANI presented the promising application in flexible energy-related device.

A schematic illustrating the fabrication process and microstructure of CQDs-PANI hybrid.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors or electrochemical capacitors, with high power density, fast charge-discharge rate and long life span, have attracted considerable attention in recent years [1]. Based on the charge storage mechanisms, supercapacitors are divided into two types, which includes electrochemical double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) and pseudocapacitors. EDLCs based on carbon materials of high surface area usually display perfect cycling stability, but low specific capacitance due to the limited electrochemically available surface area [2,3]. By comparison, pseudocapacitors based on conducting polymers and transition metal oxides present high specific capacitance, while poor cycle life [4–7]. Therefore, many research works involve the combination of the superior pseudocapacitance of conducting polymers and the good stability of carbon materials to improve the rate capability and cycling stability of the conducting polymer-based supercapacitors [8–13].

Polyaniline (PANI) is a promising electrode material due to its low cost, ease of synthesis, relatively high theoretical capacity, as well as high doping/dedoping rate during charge/discharge processes [14–16]. However, the high resistance during cycling and poor cycling stability of PANI as a result of the volumetric swelling and shrinking during ion doping-dedoping process hinder the practical application of supercapacitors [17–20]. Highly active carbon, titanium nitride and quantum dot materials have been widely investigated for photoelectrochemical applications [21–23]. In order to improve the conductivity of PANI-based electrode materials, tremendous efforts have been devoted to prepare carbon/PANI hybrid materials [24–28]. For example, a carbonaceous shell (∼5 nm) was deposited onto PANI surface via a hydrothermal reaction to enhance the cycling stability of PANI electrodes [29]. The carbonaceous shell-coated PANI achieved a capacitance retention of 95% after 10000 cycles in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte. However, the specific capacitance of the electrodes was only 189.73 F g-1. PANI was introduced onto the carbon fibers (CFs) surface via vapor deposition polymerization method. The CFs/PANI hybrid exhibited a maximum specific capacitance of 264 F g−1 [30]. Another study prepared the hybrid films of chemically converted graphene (CCG) and PANI nanofibers by vacuum filtration the mixed dispersions of both components. The supercapacitor device based on the CCG/PANI nanofibers hybrid film showed a specific capacitance of 210 F g-1 at a current density of 0.3 A g-1, and it achieved ∼79% capacitance retention after 800 cycles at 3 A g-1 [31]. Reduced graphene oxide (RGO)/PANI hybrid film was fabricated through solution processing. RGO/PANI hybrid film (906 S cm-1) shows much higher electrical conductivity than the pristine PANI (580 S cm-1) and RGO (46.5 S cm-1) [32]. Additionally, the RGO/PANI hybrid film exhibited a specific capacitance of 431 F g-1 at a current density of 0.45 A g-1 with the capacitance retention of 74% after 500 charge/discharge cycles. RGO/PANI hybrid was prepared by directly coating the PANI on the surface of RGO sheets via an in situ polymerization process. The RGO/PANI hybrid exhibited a specific capacitance of 361 F g−1 at a current density of 0.3 A g−1, with ∼82% of initial capacitance retained after 1000 cycles [33]. In most of the previous works, either the fabrication methodology is complicated or the capacitive performance of PANI-based hybrid materials is low. Therefore, electrodeposition of PANI is the preferred methodology over chemical techniques, as the former has been shown to exhibit higher specific capacitance than chemically formed PANI [34]. In addition, among various carbon materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with exceptional conducting properties that arise from the quantum confinement and edge effects [35–37] can realize the goal of improving the conductivity of PANI-based electrode materials. However, there is limited information concerning the fabrication of CQDs-PANI hybrid by electrodeposition method and application as supercapacitors electrode materials.

In this work, a novel composite electrode material of carbon quantum dots-polyaniline/carbon fibers (CQDs-PANI/CFs) was fabricated by directly photoelectrodepositing the CQDs and PANI on the surface of CFs. The CQDs were interconnected with PANI to promote electrons transportation, which could efficiently improve the conductivity of PANI and enhance its Faradic process. The unique hybrid-structured CQDs-PANI is expected to exhibit high specific capacitance, excellent rate capability and cycle stability for supercapacitor application.

The CQDs was prepared by electrooxidation of graphite according to the reported method [38], as illustrated in Fig. 1 A. Briefly, a graphite column electrode (GE) was electrooxidized at 3.0 V against a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) with a Pt counter electrode in 0.1 M KH2PO4 aqueous solution as the supporting electrolyte. The oxidant solution was then ultrasonated and ultrafiltered through a 22.0 μm filter membrane. After that, the product was washed with deionized water for three times and dried under ambient atmosphere.

The CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) hybrid was fabricated through a photoassited cyclic voltammetry (CV) electrodeposition route. Fig. 1B shows the schematic illustration of the fabrication process for CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) hybrid. In a typical procedure, CFs (3000 fibers per bunch, 10.0 cm × 0.2 cm) was cleaned with concentrated nitric acid, ethanol and distilled water, respectively, and then dried at room temperature. Subsequently, the CFs was immersed in the mixed solution containing 0.001 M CQDs, 0.1 M aniline monomer and 1.0 M sulfuric acid electrolyte (the electropolymerization process is generally conducted in acid solution (pH < 4) to get PANI with high conductivity [39–41]). After that, a photoassisted CV electrodeposition process was adopted in a standard three-electrode system using CFs as the working electrode, Pt plate as the counter electrode and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. According to reference [42], the light source was a 150 W xenon arc lamp and was placed 8 cm away from the working electrode. The potential range of −0.2–1.0 V, scan rate of 25 mV s-1 and the sweep segments of 10 cycles were applied during the CV electrodeposition, and the as-prepared hybrid was denoted as CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI). For comparison, the control samples labeled as CQDs-PANI/CFs, PANI/CFs(LI) and PANI/CFs were prepared using the identical route only omitting the light illumination, CQDs, and the light illumination, as well as CQDs, respectively.

The CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) was prepared using the same method of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) only by replacing the carbon fiber substrate with carbon paper. The control sample of PANI/CP was obtained using the same method with CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) only omitting the addition of CQDs. An acidic polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) gel electrolyte with H2SO4 and EG was prepared by a solution-casting method. PVA, H2SO4 and EG (1: 1.6: 2 in mass ratio) were dissolved in deionized water and stirred at 85 °C for 2 h. The mixed solution was continuously stirred until form a homogeneous PVA gel electrolyte. An all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor was constructed using two symmetric CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) electrodes, a porous dialysis membrane saturated with PVA gel electrolyte and the wrapped ultra-thin plastic film. In a typical process, two symmetric CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) electrode (3.0 × 1.0 × 0.06 cm) were first coated with PVA-H2SO4 gel electrolyte by spin coating and air-dried at room temperature for 0.5 h to evaporate excess water. Then, porous dialysis membrane was laid on CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) electrode and saturated with PVA-H2SO4 gel electrolyte. After that, two symmetric CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) electrodes were stacked together and wrapped with ultra-thin plastic film to form an integrated supercapacitor. The inset in Fig. 6B shows the prototype of such an all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor.

The surface morphology and microstructure of the as-prepared materials were investigated by Zeiss Ultra-Plus by Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy and JEM-2100 Transmission Electron Microscopy. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was performed on Bruker D8 Advance X-ray Diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54178 Å) operating at 40 kV and 40 Å. Fluorescence spectrum was measured using Horiba FluoroLog spectrophotometer. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area measurements were carried out with JW-BK122W volumetric sorption analyser through physical adsorption/desorption of nitrogen gas at the liquid-nitrogen temperature. Raman spectrum was performed using Renishaw Invia Reflex Raman spectrometer equipped with a He-Ne laser beam of 785 nm excitation wave in the range of 100–2000 cm−1.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) measurements and electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) of the samples were performed on a CHI760C electrochemical workstation using a three-electrode system at room temperature. The Pt plate, SCE and PANI-based electrode materials were served as the counter electrode, reference electrode and working electrode, respectively. The EIS spectra were recorded at an open potential of 0.2 V (versus SCE) with an ac-potential amplitude of 5 mV within the frequency range of 10 mHz–100 kHz. All electrolyte used for CQDs-PANI/CF(LI) was 1.0 M H2SO4 aqueous solution, while the electrolyte used for all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors using CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) electrode materials were as-prepared PVA gel electrolyte.

The as-prepared CQDs was found to exhibit a well-dispersed aqueous colloid solution (Fig. 2 A, a) after ultrasonic irradiation. Subsequently, the aqueous colloid solution was filtrated through a 22.0 μm filter membrane, as described in Refs. [36,38,43], and then obtained a long-term homogeneous phase without any noticeable precipitation at room temperature (Fig. 2A, b). The TEM image of the CQDs (Fig. 2A, c) shows that the CQDs is fairly monodisperse and has a uniform size of about 3–7 nm in diameter. Photographs of PANI/CFs, PANI/CFs(LI), CQDs-PANI/CFs and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) shown in Fig. 2B clearly revealed that a uniform and dense layer of PANI and CQDs have been coated on CFs substrate with obvious color change of cyan for PANI/CFs, blue for PANI/CFs(LI), violet for CQDs-PANI/CFs and shallow black for CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI). The distinct color change of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) (Fig. 2B, d) compared to the other samples indicates the incorporation of CQDs into PANI and the change of molecular structure and composition of PANI due to the light illumination in electrochemical polymerization process. The scheme of the formation of 3D CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) hybrid and SEM images of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) at different CV electrodeposition cycles are shown in Fig. 2C. First, nucleation process of PANI took place on CFs substrate. Meanwhile, an amount of CQDs was adsorbed on the surface of CFs via non-covalent bond, such as van der Waals interaction, electrostatic interaction or π-π stacking (Fig. 2C, a). Second, the active nucleation centers could minimize the interfacial energy barrier and followed by the vertical growth of PANI on CFs substrate. Finally, deposition of PANI occurred on the tips of the initial nuclei (Fig. 2C, b) due to the high electric field originated from edge effects. This process resulted in an extended length of PANI along the nuclei to form intertwined CQDs-PANI nanowires (Fig. 2C, c).

The microstructure of all samples was further characterized by SEM. The SEM images of PANI/CFs, PANI/CFs(LI), CQDs-PANI/CFs and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) (Fig. 3 ) show that PANI nanowires were homogeneously grown on the CFs substrate to form a three-dimensional cross-linked network. The average diameters of nanowires for PANI/CFs, PANI/CFs(LI), CQDs-PANI/CFs, CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) are 50–60 nm, 100–110 nm, 60–70 nm, 90–105 nm, respectively. Clearly, the diameters of nanowires prepared under light illumination condition (Fig. 3C, D, G, H) are much larger than that of without light illumination (Fig. 3A, B, E, F), and the outline of the former is more clear than the latter. Also, the diameters of nanowires fabricated with the addition of CQDs (Fig. 3E, F, G, H) were much of larger than that of omitting CQDs (Fig. 3A, B, C, D), indicating the CQDs were successfully introduced into the hybrids. From the high-magnification SEM images in Fig. 3F and H, it can be observed that the surfaces of the CQDs-PANI/CFs (Fig. 3F) and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) (Fig. 3H) nanowires are much rougher than the PANI/CFs (Fig. 3B) and PANI/CFs(LI) (Fig. 3D), respectively, further demonstrating the CQDs were dispersed into the hybrids. The hierarchical, 3D cross-linked network composed of nanowires and the introduction of CQDs not only could provide more ion transportation channels and reaction active sites [29,37,44,45] but also improve the conductivity of the hybrids, and thus lead to outstanding electrochemical performances of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) hybrid.

In order to confirm the successful coating of PANI, as well as to determine CQDs in CQDs-PANI/CFs and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI), XRD, Fluorescence and Raman spectra were also carried out by replacing the CFs substrate with Pt using the identical method described above. Fig. 4 A shows the XRD patterns of KH2PO4, CQDs-KH2PO4, PANI, and CQDs-PANI. The characteristic diffraction peak of CQDs was centered at 2θ = 26.48° for (002) crystalline plane, as shown in the patterns of CQDs-KH2PO4 (Fig. 4A, b) and CQDs-PANI (Fig. 4A, c). As comparison, three diffraction peaks centered at 18.9°, 20° and 26°, corresponding to (020), (002) and (200) crystalline planes of PANI are shown in the patterns of CQDs-PANI (Fig. 4A, c) and pure PANI (Fig. 4A, d). This suggests the existence of emeraldine salt form of PANI in the CQDs-PANI hybrid [36,37,40]. Fig. 4B displays the Fluorescence spectra of CQDs, PANI, PANI(LI), CQDs-PANI and CQDs-PANI(LI). The pure CQDs exhibits a luminescence peak at 418 nm [38]. While the CQDs-PANI and CQDs-PANI(LI) present two luminescence peaks at 412 nm, 434 nm and 414 nm, 434 nm, respectively, in comparison with the pure PANI and PANI(LI) at 415 nm and 434 nm. The strength of peak intensities of CQDs-PANI and CQDs-PANI(LI) is higher than that of the PANI and PANI(LI), which reflected the incorporation of CQDs into PANI [46]. Additionally, the different strength of peak intensities of the PANI(LI) and CQDs-PANI(LI) compared to the PANI and CQDs-PANI, indicates a structural and constituent modification of PANI induced by light illumination in the electropolymerization process. Raman spectroscopy is a powerful tool to characterize the microstructure of samples [47]. Fig. 4C compares the Raman spectra of CQDs, PANI, PANI(LI), CQDs-PANI and CQDs-PANI(LI). For CQDs (Fig. 4C, a), there are two dominant peaks at about 1320 cm−1 assigned to the D mode and 1602 cm−1 corresponded to the G mode of carbon [48]. While it can be observed that the CQDs-PANI (Fig. 4C, d) and CQDs-PANI(LI) (Fig. 4C, e) also exhibit two peaks at 1320 cm−1 and 1602 cm−1 that correspond to the D and G modes. However, these modes all shift to higher frequencies in comparison with that of CQDs, which is possible due to the interaction between PANI chains and CQDs [49]. Such interaction can provide strong interfacial adhesion between them and facilitate the electron transportation during redox reaction [50]. A series of peaks characteristic of PANI are presented in PANI (Fig. 4C, b), PANI(LI) (Fig. 4C, c), CQDs-PANI (Fig. 4C, d) and CQDs-PANI(LI) (Fig. 4C, e). Specifically, the bands at 421 cm−1 and 516 cm−1 are associated with out-of-plane C-H wag and the phenazine-like segment [48]. The bands of PANI at 806, 1164, 1330, 1502 and 1583 cm−1 correspond to the imine deformation, in-plane C–H bending, CN·+ stretching of the bipolaron structure, CN stretching vibrations and CC stretching vibrations of quinoid ring, respectively, suggesting the presence of an emeraldine salt state of PANI [9,51–53], which is in accordance with the XRD patterns and Fluorescence spectra. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurements have been conducted to investigate the porous structure characteristic and surface area of PANI, CQDs-PANI and CQDs-PANI (LI). Fig. 4D and E shows the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and the pore size distribution plots. It shows the feature of the type-IV isotherm [48]. The average pore size of PANI, CQDs-PANI and CQDs-PANI(LI) are 4.14, 4.04 and 4.18 nm, respectively, indicating the mesoporous structure characteristics. The BET surface areas of pure PANI, CQDs-PANI and CQDs-PANI (LI) are calculated to be 137.5, 362.4 and 538.5 m2 g−1, respectively. The corresponding pore volumes of PANI, CQDs-PANI and CQDs-PANI(LI) are 0.21, 0.53 and 0.84 cm3 g−1, respectively. Obviously, CQDs-PANI and CQDs-PANI (LI) exhibits much higher surface area and pore volume than pure PANI. Hence, the introduction of CQD increases the specific surface area as well as the pore volume of the PANI-based composites.

Electrochemical properties of CFs, CQDs/CFs, PANI/CFs, PANI/CFs(LI), CQDs-PANI/CFs and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) were evaluated by means of CV, GCD, EIS measurements using a three-electrode cell in 1.0 M H2SO4 electrolyte solution. Fig. 5 A shows CV curves of different samples in the potential range from 0 to 0.6 V versus SCE at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. As can be seen from Fig. 5A, the areas inside the CV curves of CFs (Fig. 5A, a) and CQDs/CFs (Fig. 5A, b) are very small compared to that of the hybrids (Fig. 5A, c-f). The small area corresponds to a very small specific capacitance (C
m ). Therefore, the C
m of CFs can be ignored compared to that of the hybrids electrodes. Each curve area corresponding to the CQDs-PANI hybrids electrodes is greater than that of the PANI electrode due to the enhanced capacitance originating from improved electrical conductivity. The CV curves of PANI/CFs, PANI/CFs(LI), CQDs-PANI/CFs and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) present two pairs of redox peaks (O1/R1 at 0.08/0.10 V and O2/R2 at 0.37/0.50 V), attributed to two redox transitions between the leucoemeraldine state and polaronic emeraldine state in PANI [54,55]. In addtion, the CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) hybrid electrode exhibits the larger capacitance than the CQDs-PANI/CFs, demonstrating light illumination in electrochemical polymerization process could enhance the capacitive performance of PANI, which may be related to the change of oxidation degree and ratio of benzene/quinine ring in PANI. Fig. 5B shows the GCD curves at the current density of 1.0 A g-1. Obviously, the voltage drop (IR drop) of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) is smaller than that of CQDs-PANI/CFs, PANI/CF(LI) and PANI/CF, indicating the CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) has the best conductivity that can provide rapid electron transfer to realize a fast gain/loss of charge. The specific capacitance is calculated according to Equation (1).
(1)
C m
=
I × Δ t
Δ V × m
where C
m is the specific capacitance, I is the current of discharge, Δt is the discharge time, ΔV is the potential window and m is the mass of active material. The charge/discharge time of CFs (Fig. 5B, a) and CQDs/CFs (Fig. 5B, b) is much shorter than that of the others (Fig. 5B, c-f), corresponding to the negligible capacitances similar to that indicated by the above-discussed CV curves. According to Equation (1), the specific capacitance of PANI/CFs, PANI/CFs(LI), CQDs-PANI/CFs, CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) is calculated to be 432.5 F g-1, 543.3 F g-1, 653.3 F g-1 and 738.3 F g-1, respectively (Fig. 5C). It is to be noted that the C
m of PANI/CFs(LI) and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) are 110.8 F g-1 and 85 F g-1 higher than that of the PANI/CFs and CQDs-PANI/CFs, respectively, because of the light illumination in electrochemical polymerization process. In addition, the C
m of CQDs-PANI/CFs and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) are 220.8 F g-1 and 195 F g-1 higher than the PANI/CFs and PANI/CFs(LI), respectively, which can be attributed to the synergistic effect between CQDs and PANI. The relative low capacitance of PANI/CFs is rational due to the high inherent resistance of PANI. The highest specific capacitance of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) is attributed to the effective incorporation of CQDs into PANI decreases the electron transfer resistances and increases the contact between PANI and electrolyte, and thus enhances the C
m of PANI. Table 1 lists the specific capacitances of PANI/CFs, PANI/CFs(LI), CQDs-PANI/CFs and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) at different current densities. It can be observed that the CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) possesses much larger specific capacitance than the other samples at current densities from 1.0 to 10.0 A g-1. Fig. 5C displays the specific capacitances of each sample at different current densities. The optimum capacitance of all samples is 738.3 F g−1 of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) at 1.0 A g−1, which is superior those recently reported [29,31]. The advantages of the CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) hybrid originate from the realization of rapid charge transfer, which is significantly facilitated by the introduction of CQDs into PANI and the unique 3D cross-linked nework of the PANI nanowires. To further discover the advantage of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) electrode, the rate-dependent CV over a range of scan rates from 2 to 200 mV s-1 in the potential window of 0–0.60 V versus an SCE was tested, as shown in Fig. 5D. The CV curves exhibits distinct pseudo-capacitive behavior, and the shapes can be finely maintained at relatively high scanning rate of 20 mV s-1, indicating that CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) has a rapid charge-discharge response within the potential window of 0.6 V. GCD curves of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) at different current densities of 1.0–10 A g−1 display a series symmetrical triangle curves (Fig. 5E), indicating good electrochemical reversibility of the CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) within the potential range of 0–0.60 V. The CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) hybrid electrode illustrates excellent energy (E) and power density (P), as indicated by the Ragone plots shown in Fig. 5F. The energy density and power density are calculated according to the following Equations (2) and (3).
(2)
E =
[
C m
×
( Δ V )
2
]
/ 2
(3)
P =
( I × Δ V )
/ 2 m
where E is the energy density, P is the power density, I, Δt, ΔV and m represents the same meaning with that in Equation (1). The energy density of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) can achieve a high value of 36.9 Wh kg−1 at 1.0 A g-1 and the power density is up to 0.3 kW kg-1 under the same conditions. The excellent electrochemical performance of such CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) hybrid supercapacitor can be ascribed to the following reasons: (i) the prominent specific capacitance achieves as high as 738.3 F g−1, which is beneficial for high energy density; (ii) the incorporation of CQDs into PANI endows an excellent electrical conductivity; (iii) the unique 3D conductivity network nanostructure composed of cross-linked PANI nanowires provides high specific surface area, short ion diffusion length and small charge diffusion resistance. Some additional electrochemical measurements are conducted to investigate the influence of the potential window. Fig. 5G shows the CV curves in the potential range of −6–0 V at different scan rates of 5–50 mV s−1 and GCD curves (the inset in Fig. 5G) in the potential window of −0.6–0 V at different current densities of 1.0–3.0 A g-1 for CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) electrode. The enclosed areas in the CV curves of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) in the potential range of −0.6–0 V are much smaller than that of 0–0.6 V, presenting its lower specific capacitance. This result is also elucidated by the GCD curves in the potential window of 0–0.6 V. The specific capacitance of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) in the potential window of −0.6–0 V is calculated to be 0.412 F g-1 at 1.0 A g−1, which can be ignored in comparison with 738.3 F g−1 in the potential window of 0–0.6 V. The cycling stability is another important characteristic for energy storage devices. Fig. 5H presented the cycling performance of all samples at 5.0 A g−1. The CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) presents good cycle stability and its specific capacitance retained 78% of its initial capacitance after 1000 cycles due to the incorporation of CQDs into PANI, which avoids the breakdown of the electrode material and thus benefit to a better stability. The cycling stability of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) is higher than the CQDs-PANI/CFs (77%), PANI/CFs(LI) (71%) and PANI-CFs (68%). It needs to point out that the PANI suffered from a limited cycle stability due to the destruction of the polymer long chain structure during the oxidation-reduction process [12]. In addition, the structure and chemical change of electrode after cycling stability test are examined with Raman spectroscopy. CQDs-PANI(LI) after 1000 cycles is denoted as CQDs-PANI(LI)-cycled. Fig. 5I shows Raman spectra of fresh CQDs-PANI(LI) and CQDs-PANI(LI)-cycled. The CQDs-PANI-cycled shows the weak bands of PANI at 421, 516, 806, 1164, and 1330 cm−1 when compared with fresh CQDs-PANI(LI). It means that the emeraldine salt in the CQDs-PANI(LI)-cycled is gradually decreased during continuous charge-discharge process, indicating a low doped state of PANI. There are two dominant peaks at about 1320 cm−1 and 1602 cm−1 assigned to the D-band mode and the G-band mode of carbon, indicating the conversion of sp2 hybridization to sp3 hybridization and the in-plane vibration of sp2 hybridization. The intensity ratio (D/G) suggests the defect content and order degree [9,51–53]. It can be observed that the intensity ratio of D/G of CQDs-PANI(LI)-cycled is decreased in comparison with that of fresh CQDs-PANI (LI), indicating more defect content of CQDs-PANI (LI)-cycled. This suggests that the structure and volume of CQDs-PANI(LI) electrode have been gradually decayed after the cycling test. In summary, the incorporation of CQDs can increase the conductivity of PANI-based composites, accordingly improving the capacitance and rate capability of CQDs-PANI hybrid. Additionally, the incorporation of CQDs can enhance interfacial spaces of PANI wires and alleviate the volume change of CQDs-PANI hybrid during the cycling charging/discharging process, thus improving the mechanical stability of CQDs-PANI hybrid. So, the introduction of CQD improves the electrochemical performance of PANI-based composite electrode. In addition, EIS measurements are conducted to investigate the ionic diffusion and electron transfer in the PANI-based electrodes. Fig. 5J shows the Nyquist plots of PANI/CFs, PANI/CFs(LI), CQDs-PANI/CFs and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) in the frequency range of 105–10−2 Hz. The EIS results were fitted by the equivalent circuit depicted in the inset of Fig. 5J. The equivalent circuit model included the elements of ohm resistance (Ro), constant phase element (CPE1), charge transfer resistance (Rct), and the Warburg resistance (Wo). All PANI-based electrodes show an incomplete semicircle at high frequency region and linearity at low frequency region. The incomplete semicircle at the high frequency region suggests that there is a charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the electrode/solution interface [56], while the precipitous sloping lines at the low frequency region corresponds to the Warburg resistance [57]. Table 2 lists the fitting values of the equivalent circuit elements of PANI/CFs, PANI/CFs(LI), CQDs-PANI/CFs and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI). The Ro values obtained from the intercept of the semicircle with the real axis were 2.046, 2.268, 2.272, and 2.109 Ω for PANI/CFs, PANI/CFs(LI), CQDs-PANI/CFs and CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI), respectively. Upon the modification of PANI/CFs(LI) by adding CQDs under light illumination, we observed that the fitted Rct value of the CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) electrode is 0.8175 Ω, which is much smaller than that of PANI/CFs (3.814 Ω), PANI/CFs(LI) (1.043 Ω) and CQDs-PANI/CFs (0.9560 Ω), indicating that the CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) electrode possesses the best electrochemical performance with respect to the lowest resistance of the transfer of the charge. Furthermore, the Rct of the CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) and PANI/CFs(LI) electrodes were smaller than that of CQDs-PANI/CFs and PANI/CFs, respectively, demonstrating that light illumination could enhance the charge transfer capability of PANI by forming better 3D network. The CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) also show larger value of CPE1 (0.2223) than that of PANI/CFs (0.0083), PANI/CFs(LI) (0.0132) and the CQDs-PANI/CFs (0.1578). In addition, the CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) exhibited the smallest value of Wo (0.3521 Ω), while the PANI/CFs showed the highest value of Wo (0.4310 Ω). The small value of Wo was indicative of a fast diffusion of doping and dedoping of anions occurring inside the PANI film, in accordance with the GCD results shown in Fig. 5B. These results, combining with that obtained by the CV and GCD testing, sufficiently demonstrate the promising application of CQDs-PANI/CFs(LI) hybrid in high-performance supercapacitors.

All-solid-state flexible supercapacitors are more favorable than their liquid counterparts under the consideration of encapsulation and safety, since they possess high physical flexibility, desirable electrochemical properties, and excellent mechanical integrity [58]. Therefore, all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors had been constructed based on the CQDs-PANI hybrid electrode materials. Fig. 6A shows the CV curves of the all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor based on the CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) in the potential range of −1.0–1.0 V at different scan rates of 5–20 mV s−1. The working potential from −0.6 to 0.6 V can ensure the large areas in the CV curves and reversible chemical process of PANI, thus leading to the improved the energy density. The working potential window above 1.2 V usually causes irreversible chemical process of PANI, leading to the gradually decaying capacitance performance of this symmetric supercapacitor after long-term cycles. Fig. 6B compares CV curves of the all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors based on CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) and PANI/CP electrodes at 20 mV s−1 in PVA gel electrolyte. Also, the supercapacitor prototypes of CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) is shown in the inset of Fig. 6B. Obviously, the current response and the enclosed area of CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) supercapacitor was much higher than that of PANI/CP supercapacitor, in good agreement with the CV results of CQDs-PANI/CF(LI) and PANI/CF in 1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution shown in Fig. 5A. Fig. 6C shows the GCD curves of CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) and PANI/CP supercapacitors at the current density of 1.0 mA cm−2. The specific areal capacitances of CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) and PANI/CP supercapacitors are 169.2 mF cm−2 and 84.6 mF cm−2, respectively. Moreover, the GCD curve of CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) exhibits a lower IR drop (71.0 mV) during the discharge process compared to that of the PANI/CP (94.6 mV), indicating the better conductivity [59] and thus better electrochemical performances of the CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) than PANI/CP supercapacitors. CV measurements (Fig. 6D) show that the all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors based on the CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) has almost the same CV curves at 20 mV s−1 in the planar and the bending condition (insets in Fig. 6D), which demonstrated that the CQDs-PANI/CP(LI)-based all-solid-state devices show no capacitance differences while tested in the planar and the bending states, thus the electrochemical data in this work were all obtained in the highly flexible (bending) condition. Fig. 6E shows the CV curves of the all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor based on the CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) at various scan rates from 5 to 200 mV s−1 within the potential window of −0.6–0.6 V. The CV curves are close to rectangular shapes, which are associated with the rapid response of the current to the voltage reversal. The rectangular CV curve even exists at a high scan rate of 200 mV s−1, indicating a good rate performance. The accurate electrochemical values of the all-solid-state supercapacitor based on the CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) were evaluated by the GCD measurements at different current densities, as shown in Fig. 6F. The linear profile and near symmetric triangular shape of the GCD curves suggests typical supercapacitor behavior. The specific areal capacitance was up to 169.2 mF cm−2 at 1.0 mA cm−2. The specific areal capacitance decreased to 111.4 mF cm−2 while the current density increased to 10.0 mA cm−2, that is, about 65.8% of the capacitance at 1.0 mA cm−2 was remained revealing good rate capability, which is in good accordance with its CV results (Fig. 6E). Fig. 6G displays the Ragone plot of CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) supercapacitor device. The CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) supercapacitor has a high energy density of 33.8 μWh cm−2 and a high power density of 0.6 mW cm−2. Moreover, it can still deliver an energy density of 22.3 μWh cm−2 even at a high power density of 6.0 mW cm−2, indicating a good potential for the industrial application of the highly flexible all-solid-state supercapacitors. Additionally, the individual unit of the CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) supercapacitor could operate a light-emitting-diode (LED) with an output voltage of 1.8 V, as shown in the inset in Fig. 6G. Thus, this flexible supercapacitor using CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) electrode could achieve a high specific areal capacitance, energy density and power density of 169.2 mF cm−2, 33.8 μWh cm−2 and 0.6 mW cm−2, respectively, presenting potential application in energy storage [49,60]. The electrochemical performance of the supercapacitor having the carbon paper electrodes modified with carbon quantum dots-polyaniline hybrid depends on interface resistance between the electrode and PVA-based gel electrolyte. EIS measurement was conducted to investigate the interface properties of CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) supercapacitor. Fig. 6H shows Nyquist plots of supercapacitors based on CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) electrode and PVA-H2SO4 gel electrolyte. The corresponding equivalent circuit and the enlarged Nyquist plot in high frequency range were shown in the inset of Fig. 6H. According to the fitting curve in high frequency region, CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) supercapacitor exhibits a low Ro value of 0.63 Ω and low Rct value of 0.024 Ω, indicating a good electrical conductivity of the CQDs-PANI electrode materials. In addition, the curve in the low frequency region is close to a vertical line. This implies that the supercapacitor exhibits almost ideal capacitive behavior, presenting good ion diffusion in the electrode structure [56]. Thus, the CQDs-PANI/CP(LI) supercapacitor can obtain high electrochemical performance.

A novel CQDs-PANI hybrid was synthesized by electrochemical polymerization under light illumination for supercapacitor application. It possessed a superior specific capacitance of 738.3 F g−1 at a current density of 1.0 A g−1 and the capacitance retention of 78.0% after 1000 charge/discharge cycles at the current density of 5.0 A g−1. High capacitive performance of CQDs-PANI hybrid is ascribed to the incorporation of CQDs into PANI, which improves the conductivity and alleviates the volume change during the charge/discharge process. In addition, an all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor device based on the CQDs-PANI hybrid is constructed using carbon paper as current collector and polyvinyl alcohol gel electrolyte. The CQDs-PANI supercapacitor exhibits a specific capacitance of 169.2 mF cm−2, an energy density of 33.8 μWh cm−2 and a power density of 0.3 mW cm−2 at a current density of 1.0 mA cm−2. This study suggests that CQDs-PANI hybrid is a promising candidate electrode material for flexible energy storage devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21373047), Graduate Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYLX15_0126) and Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.
